sae_features.label: Terminal helix-to-IDR tails
sae_features.description: Terminal helix-to-tail segments of small alpha‑helical proteins—most prominently histone‑fold proteins—where the feature marks the last α‑helix of the compact fold and the adjoining low‑complexity/disordered tail (often Lys/Arg‑rich in histones) that mediates DNA/RNA binding or assembly interactions; the pattern generalizes to analogous C‑terminal helix→IDR transitions in other small helical proteins

sae_features.label: Histone-fold DNA-binding segments
sae_features.description: Histone-fold and other basic nucleic-acid–binding segments: the feature primarily recognizes the histone-fold DNA-binding module and adjacent Lys/Arg-rich chromatin-contacting regions in core/variant histones, linker H1, and histone-fold subunits of NF-Y, TFIID/NC2, CENP/CHRAC, and polymerase ε complexes, but it can also activate on basic nucleic-acid–associated regions outside chromatin contexts (e.g., small basic ribosomal proteins and basic NLS/NoLS clusters in nuclear proteins).

sae_features.label: Basic N-terminal disordered tails
sae_features.description: Intrinsically disordered, Lys/Arg‑rich N‑terminal extensions and targeting peptides of nucleic‑acid–associated proteins—especially ribosomal proteins and histone N‑tails—that serve as rRNA/DNA‑contacting basic tails, nuclear/organellar localization signals (e.g., NLS or transit peptides), and PTM‑rich regulatory regions.

sae_features.label: Calmodulin-binding IQ motif helices
sae_features.description: IQ-motif–like calmodulin-binding regions: basic, amphipathic alpha‑helical stretches carrying canonical IQ motifs and arrays of IQ repeats that recruit calmodulin (often Ca2+-independent), as found in myosin necks, IQGAP/IQD scaffolds, and cytosolic regulatory tails of ion channels; the same sequence pattern also appears in centrosomal/ciliary scaffolds and some basic nuclear proteins (e.g., histone variants) that contain IQ‑like segments.

sae_features.label: Histone-like disordered tails
sae_features.description: Intrinsically disordered, low‑complexity terminal regions (especially N‑terminal tails) that flank structured DNA‑binding cores of chromatin/transcription proteins, with a strong bias toward histone‑fold proteins (i.e., histone tails and analogous IDRs used for chromatin interaction and cofactor recruitment).

sae_features.label: Histone-fold HLH hairpin detector
sae_features.description: Structural recognition of short helix–loop–helix hairpins that underlie histone-fold modules (H1–L1–H2) and histone-fold-like coiled-coil hairpins in chromatin-associated complexes; includes canonical/variant histones, histone-fold transcription factor subunits, and occasional cross-activation on bHLH and other HLH hairpins with similar geometry (e.g., TIPIN).

sae_features.label: Basic nucleic acid-binding surfaces
sae_features.description: Basic nucleic‑acid–binding interfaces: Lys/Arg‑rich and often low‑complexity patches and basic helical bundles that contact the DNA/RNA phosphate backbone in genome‑maintenance and mobile‑element proteins (e.g., histones, CRISPR nucleases, RNases, helicase accessory domains, reverse transcriptases). The feature marks nucleic‑acid interaction surfaces rather than a specific catalytic motif.

sae_features.label: Histone-fold and histone-like tails
sae_features.description: Histone-fold modules and their flanking histone-like tails across chromatin and transcription assemblies: the feature targets the three-helix histone-fold dimerization scaffold used by core/variant histones and histone-fold subunits of TFIID, SAGA/STAGA, NF‑Y, kinetochore MHF1/2, POLE3/POLE4, and viral histone doublets, often including adjacent Lys/Arg-rich (or subfamily-specific acidic) segments
